data.title: Gentex: Shifting Model Mix And A Change In Strategy Hit Sentiment
data.description:
When I last wrote about Gentex Corporation (GNTX) in mid-2024, I thought this was a relatively safe haven in a tough auto supplier space, given the company’s success in gaining share with its automatic dimming mirrors and the company’s overall track record. That was a bad call, as the company has since seen meaningful adverse changes in vehicle production mix and chose to make an odd and questionably strategic acquisition that adds meaningful non-auto exposure.

Gentex shares have lost about 30% of their value since that last article, a notably weak performance relative to other auto suppliers like Adient (ADNT), Autoliv (ALV), and Visteon (VC).

At this point, while I see value in Gentex shares, I have more concerns about the real underlying pace of advanced mirror adoption (and the value that consumers see in them), as well as management’s ability or willingness to remain focused on its core business. I can make the argument that Gentex should be near the lows of this cycle and leveraged to benefit from a shift back to production of higher trim models, but those strategy questions are an issue for me.

Q4 Likely Comes Down To Trim Mix

Gentex hasn’t been doing great of late regarding its performance against sell-side expectations, as the last two quarters have seen modest (2% to 3%) revenue misses versus analyst expectations.

A key concern remains the ongoing underperformance relative to underlying build rates. Shipments and auto revenue have been lagging underlying build rates, with the third quarter seeing an almost 7% decline in auto revenue against low single-digit underlying build growth, with North American shipments flat versus underlying production growth of 5% (OEM revenue was up 5%) and International shipments down 12% against underlying weighted volume growth of 2% (with EU OEM revenue down 14% and China revenue down 35%).

Although fourth-quarter auto-build rates were better than expected, particularly in North America, those discrepancies in recent quarters lead me to some caution on revenue in the fourth quarter. I feel better about gross margin, though, as the company has been outperforming here (even with the drag from the VOXX deal) on operational synergy and restructuring efforts, even while tariffs have been causing issues.

Net-net, then, I see fourth-quarter results as potentially coming in a little weak on the top line but coming in stronger at the operating income line.

Weaker Vehicle Mix Remains A Risk

A big part of the bull thesis on Gentex has been that the overall market for the company’s electronic auto-dimming mirrors is underpenetrated, with these mirrors making up around 35% or so of the overall market (and Gentex having somewhere in the mid-80%s market share, with others like Forvia (FAURY) and Magna (MGA) also involved to a lesser degree).

Given the safety and convenience offered by these mirrors, as well as the higher-function Full Display Mirrors (or FDMs), which include a display unit for rearview cameras, the idea was that Gentex would continue to benefit from ongoing design-ins on more and more models.

That process has stalled of late. While penetration has generally been improving in North America, Europe has seen a reversal driven by a shift to lower-trim models across multiple OEMs. This is likely largely a response to affordability issues, but it remains to be seen how quickly this will reverse. While auto affordability in the EU has improved back to pre-pandemic levels, consumers are still looking to economize, and the outlook for weak production trends in 2026 (most projections are for flat to slightly down build volumes in 2026) doesn’t bode particularly well.

China, too, remains pressured. While I don’t believe trading down in trim levels has been as significant, the market is slowing there as well, and down-trading could become a bigger issue. Moreover, tariffs and counter-tariffs have hurt the business in a meaningful way, with management highlighting this as a main driver in the weak results. Given that China has been one of the only major markets offering underlying unit growth, this remains an ongoing threat.

I’m still basically bullish on the overall adoption trend for auto-dimming mirrors, including the more advanced FDM offerings. While camera systems have gotten some attention, I think adoption will be slower than bulls expect given ongoing safety issues – when Gentex’s mirrors fail, they’re still mirrors, but when a pure camera/monitor system fails, there is no fallback (though some systems include redundant/fallback cameras). Still, as these are premium offerings, the pace of adoption is very clearly sensitive to affordability and varying consumer confidence.

How Big Of A Shift Does The VOXX Deal Represent?

The most significant change in Gentex since my last update is the acquisition of VOXX for about $200 million. VOXX has a presence in the auto supplier market through products like in-vehicle entertainment (especially rear seat entertainment systems), security, and remote start, but about 70% of the business is in consumer audio, including premium brands like Klipsch and Onkyo.

VOXX has not been doing well in the post-pandemic world, with sales falling from around $636 million in FY’22 to an annualized rate of $340 million in Gentex’s third quarter, and Gentex paid only about 0.4x sales to get the company, and I’d note that VOXX itself paid $166 million for Klipsch back in 2011.

It’s not as if this is Gentex’s first foray outside its core mirror business – businesses like HomeLink (vehicle-to-home communications systems) have been in the mix for a while, and the company acquired eSight (electronic vision enhancement technology for vision impairment) back in 2024.

Even so, I have mixed feelings about the deal. On the positive side, enhanced in-vehicle entertainment and infotainment offerings do logically fit with the company’s current strategy of value-added interior components. I can see VOXX offering some solid core technology here, and I can likewise see how security and remote start fit with the core HomeLink technology.

On the other hand, about 70% of this business is in consumer audio – a business where Gentex has no prior experience or core competencies, and where there is clearly a need to restructure and turn around the business to reignite growth and margin leverage. I suppose it is possible to just look at the consumer business as a “call option,” but valuing VOXX only on the directly relevant auto operations does mean that Gentex overpaid for a business (around 1.4x sales) that needs some improvement and doesn’t offer the sort of market share and moats that Gentex’s existing auto business has.

All in all, my biggest concern here is that VOXX’s non-auto businesses become a dilutive, value-destroying distraction for management that they end up selling later at an even lower multiple. There’s turnaround potential here, for sure, but consumer audio is a difficult market, and I need to be convinced that Gentex can turn around this business without it distracting from their growth opportunities in the auto-market.

The Outlook

Gentex has been underperforming my prior expectations for revenue and margins, and the extra margin dilution from VOXX certainly doesn’t help, nor does the outlook for another challenging year in the light vehicle market.

I’m expecting mid-single-digit revenue growth in 2026, and that could yet be optimistic if there isn’t an improvement in trim mix among EU OEMs. I’m looking for a similar 5% revenue growth rate across the next five years and a long-term annualized growth rate of around 4.5%, as I do still see growth opportunities in the company’s mirror offerings and ancillary products (like auto-dimming sunroofs and so on).

On the margin side, I think it will take some time to overcome the dilution from VOXX, and I’m expecting EBITDA margins of around 22% in FY’26 (versus recent years in the mid-20%’s and pre-pandemic margins in the low-30%’s). It’s possible that margin improvement could beat my expectations if the company can successfully renegotiate with OEMs, but given what has happened of late in terms of mix shift, it may be hard for the company to argue that their products are a must-have worthy of higher margins.

I’m expecting low-to-mid-20%s in EBITDA margin to translate into mid-teens free cash flow margins, driving around 6% long-term FCF growth. Again, if the company can do a better job of lifting margins in VOXX and/or secure margins from auto OEMs similar to what they got five or 10 years ago, there’s upside here.

Between discounted cash flow and margin-driven multiples-based valuation, Gentex does look undervalued, with the former giving me a fair value of around $29 and the latter giving me a range of $25 to $28 (with a 1.9x forward revenue multiple and/or a 10x forward EBITDA multiple).

The Bottom Line

There are still things to like about Gentex, including its strong market share in a market that still offers meaningful above-build growth potential, good margins, and solid mid-to-high-teens ROIC. I also like the active share repurchase program and the clean balance sheet.

If management can successfully integrate and improve VOXX, and if consumers go back to demanding vehicles with more bells and whistles, I can see Gentex outperforming. I think the shares are likely to remain relatively limited absent stronger above-build-rate volumes, though, and until there’s more comfort around the VOXX deal. The valuation makes this a name to watch, but I don’t think there’s a need to rush right now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kodiak AI: Competition And Dilution Risk Undermine The Bull Case
data.description:
Kodiak AI, Inc. (KDK) is a developer of autonomous driving technology for trucks that is differentiated by its decision to focus on off-road applications. While it is difficult to compare most self-driving technology companies based on publicly available information, it appears that Kodiak is trailing Aurora Innovation (AUR), and this is reflected in the market capitalizations of the two companies.

The market is still nascent though, and a real leader is yet to be established. As a result, Kodiak's stock could perform well over the next few years if the company can demonstrate the viability of its technology on public roads. This is supported by Kodiak's relatively low cash burn rate and the fact that revenue from off-road operations should start to scale in 2026.

Market

While the adoption of self-driving trucks will eventually be dictated by cost, labor shortages and a potential safety advantage are also reasons to be optimistic about the development of the market in coming years. Human drivers currently cost roughly $1 USD per mile, leading to around $340,000 USD in average annual expenses. Kodiak can eliminate this cost, as well as reducing driver-related overheads and fuel costs.

While the safety aspect still needs to be demonstrated in practice, the fact that over 85% of truck crashes are caused by human error suggests there will be a sizeable advantage long-term. Improved safety will also help transport companies to manage insurance costs, which have risen significantly in recent years. In addition, self-driving trucks facilitate 24 hour operations and will help the roughly 70% of retailers that are targeting next-day or same-day delivery.

Autonomous trucking is an enormous opportunity, even if this isn't really reflected in the market capitalizations of the leading public companies at the moment. The global commercial long-haul trucking TAM alone is over $4 trillion USD, and the opportunity in the U.S. is in excess of $900 billion USD. There is also a $68+ billion USD industrial opportunity and a $150+ billion USD defense opportunity, which is predominately in the U.S.

Kodiak has chosen to initially focus on predominantly off-road opportunities, which could be seen either as an intelligent way of scaling revenue while the technology develops, or an attempt to mask weakness. While I tend to think it is probably a little of each, there is no real reason to believe that the performance of Kodiak's driver won't soon converge with the likes of Aurora.

Self-driving technology could quickly become a commodity if more than a handful of companies introduce viable services. While there is an opportunity for companies to differentiate on cost and safety, I believe that this will be difficulty based on how the market appears to be developing. In addition, there is mounting evidence from other areas that the performance of leading AI models tends to converge over time. A monopoly or a market dominated by 2-3 large players therefore appears unlikely, and as a result, the economics of self-driving technology could be worse than widely anticipated.

Aurora Innovation is one of the better positioned competitors, due to the fact that it has already deployed self-driving trucks on public roads. The company also has a strong balance sheet and relatively large market capitalization, enabling it to invest in growth. While Aurora has driverless trucks on the road, the fact that it isn’t rapidly scaling operations on the Dallas to Houston route suggests this isn’t a solved problem yet.

Kodiak's solution is differentiated by the fact that it does not rely on HD maps, which is critical for off-road operations. Kodiak also hasn't chosen to develop its own sensors, which is an important reason why its cash burn is modest relative to Aurora.

Of note, Aurora plans on commencing autonomous highway operations for Detmar Logistics, hauling proppant between a sand mining site and Detmar's facility in the Permian Basin. Detmar has committed to using 30 Aurora Drivers in 2026, each transporting sand for 20+ hours per day. This could be considered a direct threat to Kodiak, but even within proppant hauling the two companies are pursuing slightly different opportunities.

Plus AI is an American autonomous driving technology company focused on commercial freight trucking. Its PlusDrive solution is initially intended for use with a driver in the vehicle, and can be retrofitted into commercial freight trucks. The company had carried out road testing of its technology in 48 states in the U.S. by the end of 2021. The company also has operations in China, although Plus separated its U.S. and Chinese operations into independent companies in 2023. Plus is partnered with the likes of Bosch and IVECO on the supply side and previously received an order for 1,000 self-driving systems from Amazon. A commercial launch isn't expected until 2027 though. The company plans on going public through a SPAC in the near future, which is expected to raise around $300 million USD at roughly a $1.2 billion USD valuation.

Waabi (private) is another self-driving technology developer that is focused on the freight market. Waabi was founded in 2021 by the former chief scientist of Uber ATG. Its key technologies are the Waabi Driver and the Waabi World simulation platform. Waabi partnered with Uber Freight in 2023, and has regularly been operating between Dallas and Houston since. The company began expanding its operating domain from highways to surface streets in 2024. This is important because more than 40% of the miles traveled by freight trucks are on surface streets. Waabi plans on expanding to other lanes in Texas and beyond, but appears to be behind the likes of Aurora as it only completed fully driverless testing on a closed-course in October.

The relative importance of surface streets suggest that robotaxi companies could successfully adapt their technology to address the freight market. Waymo is perhaps the best positioned candidate to do this, although its trucking initiative was paused in 2023. The decision appears to have been driven by a desire to limit cash burn and the fact that Waymo's robotaxi business had matured to the point that it was ready to scale rapidly. I expect Waymo to restart its freight business in the next few years, and it should grow quickly when it does.

There has also been talk of an autonomous Tesla (TSLA) truck since the Tesla Semi was first announced in 2017. Consumer deliveries of the Semi started in 2022, and PepsiCo (PEP) now has a fleet of almost 100 vehicles. While the Tesla Semi is now on the road in small numbers, it doesn't feature full self-drive, or FSD, at the moment. Tesla is likely to combine its self-driving technology with the Semi platform in the future. The company's decision not to utilize LiDAR or radar could be more of an issue in trucking, though, due to a combination of vehicle weight and speed.

Kodiak AI

Kodiak AI is a self-driving technology company that was founded in 2018 to pursue the autonomous trucking opportunity. The company began hauling freight in 2019 and is operating out of Dallas. Since 2022, Kodiak has been targeting the defense market, which aligns with its initial focus on off-road applications. While it would be difficult to characterize Kodiak as the market leader, it has been operating fully driverless vehicles for Atlas since December 2024.

Detail on Kodiak’s AV system is limited, but it appears to be broadly similar to the technology being developed by the likes of Aurora. Kodiak makes use of parallel deep neural networks and has a proprietary multimodal AI perception model, Giga FusionNet. In terms of sensors, the company leverages cameras, radar and LiDAR.

Kodiak’s technology can operate at day or night and in a wide range of weather conditions, including rain and dust storms. It is also able to operate without high-definition maps, which should support scaling. Kodiak's modular autonomy hardware kit can be adapted to a wide range of vehicles (Class 8 trucks, F-150s, military vehicles) and its sensor pods are designed to be easy to swap in the field. This could be important in coming years, if and when Kodiak seeks to expand beyond trucking.

The deployment of Kodiak's driver is supported by Vay’s remote assistance technology, which is used to provide human judgement, enhancing safety and expanding Kodiak’s operating envelope. Remote support is important for tasks like launching and landing at customer facilities and edge cases like interpreting law enforcement signals. There is little publicly available information about the intensity of support currently needed for self-driving trucks, but presumably the burden is low relative to robotaxis.

Kodiak's AV system is still under development, with updates both driving performance gains and expanding Kodiak's operating envelope. This includes new software that was released at the start of Q4, reducing the need for remote assistance by 53%. The update also introduced a vision language model that helps Kodiak's driver to manage edge cases, which can be difficult for more traditional perception techniques. Kodiak also added the ability to haul 2 trailers at the same time.

Kodiak introduced an Autonomy Readiness Measure in the third quarter, similar to Aurora. ARM measures the percentage of safety case claims that are materially complete. Kodiak’s ARM is 100% in industrial and 78% in long-haul, indicating the company still has significant work to do before it launches commercial long-haul operations. This primarily relates to validation and testing of the system. Even though there is still significant work remaining, Kodiak expects to commence commercial long-haul operations in the second half of 2026.

The impact of Kodiak's system on safety has also been independently assessed by Nauto, a fleet safety technology company. Kodiak received a VERA (Visually Enhanced Risk Assessment) safety score of 98, the highest among 1,000 fleets in Nauto's network (the average VERA Score for a Nauto-equipped fleet is 78). VERA is a proprietary safety benchmark created from over 20 driving variables covering areas like attentiveness, driving behavior and traffic violations. VERA scores range from 1 to 100, with 100 being the safest. Data shows that every 10-point reduction in the VERA score is associated with a 21% reduction in collision rate.

While Kodiak doesn't have fully driverless trucks on public roads yet, it has been conducting operations across 23,000 miles of autonomous lanes. This includes operations between Dallas and Atlanta for J.B. Hunt (JBHT) (over 295,000 miles traveled), and operations between Dallas and Jacksonville and Dallas and Atlanta for Werner (roughly 300,000 miles traveled).

Geographic expansion is supported by a regulatory environment that is becoming increasingly favorable. In particular the Department of Transportation has issued a waiver allowing self-driving trucks to use flashing warning beacons when stopped as a replacement for reflective warning triangles.

Kodiak's ability to scale production of trucks equipped with its self-driving system is likely to come into sharper focus in 2026 due to the Atlas relationship. Contract manufacturers assemble Kodiak’s AV system (sensor pods, compute, ACE) and Roush installs the AV system into OEM vehicles, which enables scale and improves quality relative to in-house production. Roush launched a dedicated manufacturing line in Q3 and the first Roush built driverless vehicles were delivered to Atlas in the quarter.

On the customer front, Kodiak's business is currently dominated by its relationship with Atlas Energy Services. Atlas had 10 operational trucks at the end of Q3 delivering day and night in most weather conditions. The company has also made an initial commitment for 100 trucks, with the larger order coming after Kodiak exceeded key performance and operational milestones in early 2025.

Kodiak has also had some success in the defense vertical, although this work appears to be at the pilot stage so far though. Kodiak entered into a contract with the U.S. Army in 2022 to develop an end-to-end autonomous solution for the Robotic Combat Vehicle Program. Testing has been completed in real-world environments and Kodiak received $30 million USD for the 3-year program. While the Army program was completed in 2025, Kodiak believes that autonomy will eventually be integrated into all defense vehicles and continues to pursue opportunities in this space.

Kodiak is also collaborating with Textron systems in defense, with Kodiak providing autonomous technology for Textron’s vehicles. For example, Kodiak's AV system has been integrated into the RIPSAW M3, which has no room for a driver.

Financial Analysis

While Kodiak's technology is still under development, the company's partnership with Atlas means that revenue is already starting to scale. The company had deployed 10 customer-owned driverless trucks at the end of the third quarter, and has now delivered over 10,000 loads and driven more than 3 million autonomous miles.

Kodiak generated $0.8 million USD revenue in the third quarter, a 53% sequential increase, with growth driven by DaaS revenue. The company expected to end 2025 with a mid to high teen number of customer owned and operated driverless trucks, with the deployment of trucks for Atlas likely to drive strong revenue growth in coming quarters.

Customers own the vehicles, which are operated by Kodiak as a service. Kodiak expects something like $150-300 thousand USD annual revenue per vehicle, dependent on assumptions and business model (per mile fee of license fee per vehicle). This likely means that revenue in 2026 will be in the high single digit or low double-digit million dollar range.

Kodiak's GAAP operating loss in Q3 was $30 million USD, while its non-GAAP operating loss was $24.7 million USD and free cash flow was negative $40 million USD. Kodiak ended Q3 with $146.2 million USD in cash and cash equivalents, meaning it will need to raise addition capital in the next 1-2 years. Given the company's current market capitalization, the dilution necessary to finance the business through to breakeven is likely to be material.

Conclusion

Kodiak AI recently went public via a SPAC, but it is yet to really capture the imagine of investors. This isn't really surprising, as the self-driving space has largely been shunned by the market, excluding Tesla.

Kodiak still needs to demonstrate fully driverless capabilities on public roads, and additional capital will be needed to finance the business through to breakeven. If this can be achieved, Kodiak could generate strong returns for investors. For example, analysts are currently projecting around $2.2 billion USD revenue in 2034, against a current market capitalization of approximately $1.7 billion USD.

I believe that it is difficult to predict the likely future market structure and competitive landscape though, making it difficult to have confidence in any particular company. In addition, Kodiak's revenue won't really begin to scale until at least 2027/2028, leaving investors exposed to significant downside in the meantime.

While the deployment of fully driverless vehicles on longer routes could be a catalyst in 2026, it should be kept in mind that this event did little for Aurora's share price. There is a significant valuation discrepancy between the two companies though, and this is likely to close somewhat if and when Kodiak commences commercial long-haul operations.

data.title: SCHD: Modeling Expected Changes For The March 2026 Reconstitution
data.description:
Introduction

This article previews possible upcoming changes to the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), whose Index is set to reconstitute effective March 23, 2026. Using the Index's published methodology, I model a hypothetical post-rebalance portfolio and present its aggregate fundamentals and sector mix, which I hope will assist readers in deciding whether it's worth waiting until March to buy or if they should consider planning an exit strategy. I've assigned SCHD a neutral "hold" rating to reflect this uncertainty, but I am optimistic about these forecasted changes, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

SCHD Overview

SCHD tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index, selecting stocks that have paid dividends for at least ten straight years and whose market caps are above $500 million. REITs are not included, nor are the remaining stocks that rank in the bottom 50% by indicated dividend yield. The Index selects the top 100 stocks based on a composite scoring system that combines dividend yield, dividend growth, return on equity, and free cash flow-to-debt, while allowing current components that rank within the top 200 to remain. Lastly, the Index weights are based on float-adjusted market cap with a 4% security and 25% sector cap, with annual reconstitutions effective after the third Friday in March.

SCHD investors appreciate its excellent mix of dividend yield and dividend growth. Along with VYM, VIG, and DGRO, SCHD is one of four large-cap value ETFs on a 10+ year dividend growth streak. When combined with its 3.77% estimated dividend yield, 9.15% five-year annualized dividend growth rate, 0.06% expense ratio, and $73.0B in net assets, it's easy to see its appeal. At the same time, critics point out that SCHD's 3Y and 5Y total returns through December 2025 were relatively soft, assessing that its defensive tilt and focus on dividends are not worth the sacrifice in capital appreciation potential.

SCHD Forecasted Sector-Level Changes

The model I built to forecast possible changes to SCHD's portfolio in March closely follows its Index's published methodology, though it's not perfect. My data source reports certain fundamental statistics slightly differently, and dividend yields won't be finalized until the end of February, leaving room for a few borderline stocks like IBM (IBM) and Cisco Systems (CSCO) to qualify. However, even with these limitations, I'm confident SCHD's Energy sector exposure will be meaningfully reduced by about 8%. Here's what I expect from a sector allocations perspective:

I anticipate Halliburton (HAL) will slightly miss the median yield screen, while Valero Energy (VLO) and Ovintiv (OVV) should exit due to large annual declines in ROE. Other Energy stocks like SLB N.V. (SLB) and ConocoPhillips (COP) will see their allocations reduced to align with their current market caps.

I also expect Financials to receive a 5% boost in total exposure, led primarily by the addition of Blackstone Group (BX). Other possible adds are CME Group (CME) and The Progressive Corp. (PGR).

SCHD Changes: ROE And Dividend Growth Are The Statistics That Matter

As I mentioned earlier, many investors buy SCHD for its excellent mix of dividend yield and dividend growth. However, few analysts closely examine the Index's four fundamental screens, and even fewer realize the sensitivity of each input into the composite scoring system. Having closely replicated the ranking model, I can confidently say that ROE and dividend growth are the key statistics that will matter in March.

To illustrate, consider the following table that lists these four statistics for the top 25 stocks I forecast SCHD to be underweight in March relative to its current portfolio. I've highlighted Valero Energy (VLO) as a nice example - its 5.24% ROE is far below the current portfolio's 27.57% average, and down from the 10.73% ROE I reported last March. In total, these stocks have a 21.96% weighted average ROE, which is about 6% below the current average and 8% below the forecasted average of 29.65%.

That's not to say the other statistics don't matter. The table also reveals that these underweight positions yield 3.50% compared to the current 3.83% average. In addition, they've collectively increased their dividends per share by 7.96%, which is 0.60% below the current average. However, these impacts are smaller than ROE's impact. Notably, there's hardly any variance in the debt as a percentage of free cash flow figures.

In contrast, dividend growth is the driving factor when it comes to SCHD's forecasted additions. As shown below, the top 25 overweighted positions have increased their dividends by an annualized 11.23% over the last five years in aggregate, 2.67% more than the current average. In particular, Blackstone Group's (BX) 16.69% 5Y Dividend CAGR benefits from a favorable base year of 2020, when its dividend growth was flat after declining by 21% the year prior.

We also see these positions yield 3.90%, which is a slight improvement over the Index's current 3.83% figure. ROE is essentially a wash (27.18% VS. 27.57%), while these overweight positions marginally reduced the fund's debt as a percentage of free cash flow.

Quick Summary: On March 23, 2026, expect SCHD to delete companies whose profits, as measured by ROE, have materially declined over the past year, and increase exposure to companies with strong dividend growth track records and marginally better yield and debt ratios.

A Brief Word About ROE

ROE is structurally a more sensitive capital efficiency ratio than ROTC (return on capital), which may lead SCHD to experience significant year-over-year changes despite no changes to a company's underlying business strength and dividend fundamentals. The reason is twofold:

1. ROE has a smaller denominator than ROTC (equity vs. debt + equity). Ratios with smaller denominators are more sensitive to absolute changes in the numerator.

2. ROE uses net income excluding discontinued operations and extraordinary items, while ROTC uses operating income. Therefore, ROTC excludes non-operational items like asset write-downs and foreign exchange adjustments by design, whereas ROE does not.

Other items are sometimes questionable. For example, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) reported a $12.4B change in legal expenses in 2025, which helped boost its ROE, as reported by Seeking Alpha, to 33.62% vs. 20.06% last March. SCHD's Index excludes "extraordinary items" from its basic EPS calculation, but it's not clear if the Index excludes these types of items.

My modeling indicates that if the less-sensitive ROTC were used instead of ROE, Philip Morris (PM) would likely replace Blackstone Group (BX), and SCHD's total expected Financial sector exposure would be cut in half to 7%. The reason is that ROE rewards leveraged sectors like Financials, while ROTC strips out that bias and may be better for cross-sector comparisons.

SCHD Forecasted Fundamentals

Next, I'd like you to consider the following fundamental differences between SCHD's expected and current portfolio as well as VYM, VIG, and DGRO. Please note the dividend yield figures are slightly different than those presented above because this table includes the entire net effect of all changes, whereas the previous analysis considered only the top 25 over- and underweights.

Here are three takeaways to consider.

1. This model predicts that SCHD's dividend profile will be largely unchanged. After adjusting out the fund's 0.06% expense ratio, SCHD's estimated dividend yield is 3.82% at current prices, and its constituents have increased their dividends per share by an annualized 7.95% over the last five years. SCHD's 57.26% payout ratio is elevated versus its peers, and although this isn't unusual for a high-dividend ETF, it's a safety ratio that I like to watch closely. Dividend math tells us that company payout ratios will increase if earnings do not grow sufficiently to support dividend increases. That's a big issue with SCHD's current iteration, as its holdings' 3Y EPS CAGR is -7.33%, but the expected portfolio's 0.14% rate indicates the problem may moderate soon.

2. Most exciting for me is a meaningful increase in the expected portfolio's one-year estimated EPS growth rate (5.48% vs. 0.94%). To my earlier point, this is much-needed relief, and when evaluating the growth rates in the tables below, the reason is obvious. The portfolio's ten largest forecasted overweighted stocks collectively have an 11.54% one-year estimated EPS growth rate, while analysts expect EPS to decline by 2.98% for the top ten underweighted positions.

To be sure, SCHD's estimated EPS growth rate still trails VYM, VIG, and DGRO by 5-8%. However, it might be enough to overcome the psychological constraint investors have about betting on companies whose businesses are in decline. I don't have an exact threshold number to provide you with, but I believe there's a point where many investors won't touch companies that aren't growing, no matter how attractive they are on other factors like yield, value, and quality.

3. On that note, SCHD's forecasted portfolio excels on ROE (31.56%) vs. its peer group, and it's even competitive on ROTC (13.66%). Importantly, both statistics represent an improvement while keeping the fund's P/E ratios firmly on the low end of the large-cap value space. My model predicts some valuation ratios will increase (e.g., P/S, P/B), but from an earnings-based valuation perspective, it's at least neutral.

Summary and Conclusion

I wrote this article to prepare SCHD investors about possible changes to the portfolio in March. While I'm confident my modeling is accurate, it is subject to error, and for this reason, I don't believe anything other than a "hold" rating is appropriate. However, modeling SCHD's methodology revealed the sensitivity of its ROE screen and led me to question its appropriateness in cross-sector comparisons, specifically as it relates to the Financial sector. ROE may also lead to significant year-over-year changes in riskier sectors like Energy without improving the portfolio's overall dividend yield and growth profile, which I believe is SCHD's main attraction. For this reason, I intend to place more emphasis on ROTC as a more stable measure of quality moving forward.

Meaningfully improving the portfolio's growth profile would be a huge win for a fund that's struggled to participate in the 3+ year bull market. If my forecast is correct, SCHD will have done that without impacting its advantages on dividend yield, dividend growth, valuation, and quality. Therefore, I think these are the changes SCHD needs right now, and I look forward to circling back on March 23, 2026, to assess the prediction and evaluate the actual holdings in closer detail. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Agnico Eagle: Not The Best Gold Mining Pick Due To Valuation And ROE Concerns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM), the second largest gold stock by market cap, is an excellent company, but while the business is solid, the valuation is somewhat stretched relative to other large peers. Agnico Eagle has been an underperformer among the largest peers, albeit with a whopping +133% in total returns in the last twelve months, pushed higher by a gold price rally last year. However, on a comparative basis, the stock underperformed by 60% compared to Newmont’s 193% and by 85% in relation to Barrick’s 218% total return in the last twelve months.

Of course it’s still a great performance, but that is primarily due to significant sector tailwinds, which are now possibly in the rearview mirror, with some USD momentum to the upside and gold price resistance at $4,600/ounce which remains unbroken since October. My Hold rating means that while gold tailwinds could continue pushing the stock higher, I do think investors are better off holding Newmont Corporation (NEM) (my personal favorite for 2026 to which I gave a Strong Buy rating a few days ago) or even Barrick Mining Corporation (B) (which I rated a Hold in case gold stalls in 2026. Indeed, these two stocks trade at a lower PE than AEM, and while I prefer Newmont, Barrick also has the potential Fourmile upside surprise.

It is worth noting that Agnico was clearly leading the rally in the first half of 2025 and lost significant momentum in the last quarter of 2026, precisely the quarter where the gold price started trading within a price range rather than continuing to soar upwards. We will dive right in, but the company noted some free cash flow impact after, in my view, excessive capex spending and lagging ROE.

Hard Figures

Now diving into the hard figures, Agnico is a solid company but a comparatively bigger spender. From a balance sheet perspective, the company is solid with a large cash pile of $2.3 billion and total assets of $32.7 billion, far surpassing total liabilities of $9.2 billion. Despite a large cash pile, the company only pays a 0.82% dividend yield (forward payment of $1.60 per share) so the dividends are clearly not an argument to hold onto the stock and not an attractive target for dividend investors.

The growth rates are also very respectable, with a revenue growth rate of +35.16% on a YoY basis based on Q3 figures, powered by a strong gold price rally in the first three quarters of last year. Operating cash flow also rose 64.12% YoY.

But there is some weakness. Indeed, capex increased by almost 30%. In contrast, Newmont also boasts strong growth rates (26.59% revenue growth and 106.45% operating cash flow growth) but with much more efficient capex spending, given Newmont’s management managed to decrease capex spending by -10% while generating more revenue and operating income. The higher capex spending is causing Agnico to show some signs of weakness with a drop in FCF from Q2 to Q3 2025 according to their Q3 earnings report, despite reaching a record FCF on a YTD basis.

Higher capex does not seem to favor profitability, as the figures are practically identical between Agnico Eagle Mines and Newmont, with +/- 33% net income margin for both, despite a more favorable capex trend for Newmont. Newmont boasts a significantly higher ROE at 22.84% against Agnico’s 15.67%. In other words, Newmont manages to grow and stay highly profitable with more efficient equity and capex allocation.

Comparative Valuation

In my view, the higher efficiency of Newmont should warrant a valuation premium relative to Agnico. But despite a 60% stock underperformance in 2025 relative to Newmont, Agnico is still trading at a premium that remains unjustified in my opinion. Indeed, Agnico trades at 24x 2025 earnings and 18x 2026 earnings, at a 33% and 28% premium relative to its larger (and more efficient) competitor Newmont. Lastly, from a Price to Sales perspective, Agnico is trading 9x, significantly higher than its historical multiple closer to 4-5x and Newmont’s current 5x. I am using P/S due to the volatility of earnings of firms which have earnings strongly tied to the price of a commodity. Using P/S gives a more stable valuation picture. But even from an EV/EBITDA perspective, the stock is still trading at a premium at 14x while Newmont is trading at 10x.

I believe there will be a stock repricing across the sector, favoring more efficient scale of operations and capex spendings, especially if gold price stalls. And while last year all the gold miners won, 2026 could be a stock picker’s year in the sector with gold predicted by the larger banks to trade in the $4,500-5,000 per ounce for the remaining part of the year

The stock has by the way a Seeking Alpha valuation grade of C- compared to Newmont’s B-. If gold price stalls or increases moderately, the markets will be more careful in picking their winners, especially after the rally we witnessed last year amongst gold miners.

Factors That Could Alter My Hold Rating

Two factors that would realistically alter my Hold rating into a Buy or a Strong Buy would be the discovery of a large gold deposit like Barrick’s Fourmile discovery (deemed one of the discoveries of the century) by Agnico Eagle, or if there is clear evidence of an improvement in ROE in relation to Newmont’s with less capex spending, higher FCF, and preserved or improved profitability. I will pay very close attention to these metrics in the next earnings report release relative to Q4 2025, a quarter where gold traded within a price range of $4,200-4,500. Of course, if the stock price dips significantly on nonmaterial news, that could allow for an interesting entry or accumulation point as long as the fundamentals remain solid.

On the other end, weakness in gold price and shareholder dilution are factors that could alter my rating towards a Sell or even Strong Sell. Just like Newmont, Agnico Eagle Mines is heavily dependent on demand for gold, deriving more than 90% of its revenue from this precious metal. Gold price could experience weakness due to lower demand or sudden oversupply, for example, from new discoveries such as Barrick Mining’s Fourmile, which still requires time to be developed but could lead down the line to shocks in supply. For now, rising demand continues to be higher than supply. The management is obviously aware of its strong dependence on gold and is looking to diversify into other metals. However, diversification sometimes leads to worsification in case the business becomes distracted by other objectives that are not in its immediate domain of expertise or specialization. The company announced acquiring shares of a Canadian company (without controlling it) that explores and sets up mining operations of copper and zinc. Osisko’s undeveloped assets in Canada have potential, but short-term acquisitions like these can typically lead to shareholder dilution and can be distractions from the core segment that is growing right now: gold mining. It’s worth keeping an eye on the M&A and understanding if synergies are achieved on new acquisitions. A point I will monitor very closely in the next earnings reports is how the company spends the cash that it is generating while the tailwinds in gold last.

Bottom Line

Agnico Eagle Mines is certainly a great company, but the valuation seems stretched with some early signs worth investors’ attention in relation to the firm’s capex and free cash flow. Agnico Eagle Mines may outperform the broader market thanks to strong sector tailwinds if gold prices continue to soar in 2026, but it may significantly underperform its large peers such as Newmont, especially if gold prices take a breather and stock pickers return to the steering wheel in the sector.

data.title: To Cut Or Not To Cut: What's Next For The U.S. Fed
data.description:
Even amid signs of cooling inflation, Gennadiy Goldberg, Head of U.S. Rates Strategy at TD Securities, expects the Federal Reserve to hold rates at its January meeting, though we could see cuts at subsequent meetings. He joins MoneyTalk to lay out his outlook for rates in 2026.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: Well, the latest US inflation report showed more signs of cooling. But does it mean cuts may be on the table in their decision later this month? Joining us now to discuss is Gennadiy Goldberg, head of US rates strategy with TD Securities.

Gennadiy, welcome to the show.

Gennadiy Goldberg: Thanks for having me.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. So let's start off by getting your take on the morning's inflation report. What do you see there?

Gennadiy Goldberg: Well, lots of signal, a lot of noise as well. Unfortunately, we're coming off of a couple of months of very shutdown impact data. From what we can tell, this is a slightly better report, all on net. I'll give you the bottom line first. It really tells us that inflation is starting to moderate in the United States. It's not coming up as much as people expected. And going forward, what really matters-- obviously, aside from the inflation prints-- is going to be the labor market.

Now, the Fed can look to this inflation print and really say, OK, well, inflation is not materially accelerating yet. Maybe it will be accelerating at some point in the future. We really have to wait and see. So in the short term, this really means that the labor market is going to be a lot more in focus over the next couple of months.

Can they cut rates in January? We think unlikely. But the market is pricing in relatively low odds of a cut in March. And of course, that can't be ruled out just yet. This is going to be data-dependent. Every meeting is live. Every data point is going to matter. And it is going to be a lot of repricing for markets to do.

Anthony Okolie: Again, you mentioned those rate cuts. How does that set it up for the rate decision? You said that you don't think that the Fed is likely going to cut in January.

Gennadiy Goldberg: Yeah, you know the market's pricing in very low odds of a January cut-- just about a basis point out of those 25 basis points. So really low odds-- has actually declined even further after payrolls, from really couple of basis points to just about a basis point. But the market's still penciling in somewhere between two and three cuts for the rest of the year. So call it just shy of 75 basis points. We're actually looking for three rate cuts. We expect to cut in March, June, and September as the Fed dials in where they think neutral is, which we think is around 3%.

So we still think they have enough fodder to actually continue to cut rates over the course of the year. They're just in no rush. Inflation doesn't seem to be coming down dramatically, but it's also not rising. The labor market seems to be a little bit on the softer side, and it is softening over time. But again, it's not falling off a cliff. So it really allows them to be in this wait-and-see type of environment for even longer.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. Of course, now we're just one week into the new year. And the big headlines, of course, is around the Fed right now-- the US Justice Department probing Fed Chair Powell. How should investors be viewing this news?

Gennadiy Goldberg: You know, the initial reaction was relatively negative. Interest rates sold off. The curve actually steepened, really on the expectation that an erosion of Fed independence, which is quite crucial for any central bank, would lead to higher inflation down the line-- may need more rate cuts in the near term if somebody takes over who's more dovish. But it means that maybe the central bank is not quite as credible on their inflation-fighting capacity.

That's really changed. Powell came out swinging. He didn't have to release a video. He could have just simply released a statement. He called it an attack on central bank independence. He said there was ulterior motive. So, at the end of the day, Powell has materially pushed back. It's garnered a lot of support within Congress, from former Fed chairs, from former Treasury secretaries.

So really, it's kind of backfired on the administration, where the Fed is actually pushing back materially. And I think markets are liking the fact that that pushback suggests maybe even more Fed independence down the line. It's going to have to be something we watch.

We've got the Lisa Cook trial next week-- that's Governor Cook. The Trump administration has been trying to push her out of her seat. If that's successful, that could erode Fed independence as well. So there's a lot more to watch on this front. But for now, the markets seem a little bit more sanguine, even though this is really an attack on Fed independence and Fed credibility.

Anthony Okolie: I think there's a lot of concern about that independence. Do you think that this situation could have any potential implications for rates, despite the pushback from Fed's Chairman Powell?

Gennadiy Goldberg: Absolutely. If we see the central bank-- excuse me, the Supreme Court argument really moving in the way of the prosecution, the Trump administration, it could really start to pressure interest rates higher. The worry is, Governor Cook was accused of mortgage fraud. The administration was trying to remove her from office just based on those accusations. And the question in front of the Supreme Court is, do they have the capacity to do that?

If the Supreme Court rules no, or they're critical of the administration's approach, I think it would reinforce Fed independence and I think the markets would breathe a sigh of relief. If it looks like they're siding with the administration, if they seem to be conducive to those arguments, it could actually make markets worried that perhaps not just Governor Cook is on the firing line, but so is Chair Powell, and so is the rest of the FOMC board.

And whoever replaces them will be much more keen to actually take interest rates sharply lower, whether it's justified or not. That could send inflation expectations sharply higher. And it would probably send long interest rates sharply higher as well.

Anthony Okolie: Okay, so getting back to the inflation report, well, of course we had last week's jobs report. How's the economy looking right now? And again, what are you expecting for rates through 2026?

Gennadiy Goldberg: So, I'll start with the last question first. We are expecting rates to gradually decline. That is based on our expectation that over the course of the year, the economy will continue to gradually moderate. We're not looking for a recession. But we are looking for a little bit of slowing down on the labor market front, enough to keep the Fed a little bit nervous.

We're also looking for inflation on a year-on-year basis to finish the year at about 2 and 1/2%, so actually lower than we are today. And that would encourage the Fed to get rates back towards the 3% neutral level, which we think really is their target.

So we're not looking for a recession. We're not looking for a re-acceleration in the economy, as well. A little bit of a "steady as she goes" type of economy with risks tilted to the downside, which again tilts the risks, the rates to the downside as well. We're still looking for the 10-year to finish the year at about 3 and 1/2%, so well below market consensus.

Original Post

data.title: How The Ailing Multi-Family Sector Impacts The U.S. Economy
data.description:
Much of the coverage around the commercial real estate [CRE] market over the past few years has been around the cratering office sector. This makes sense, as thanks to the explosion of the virtual workforce following Covid and other factors, vacancy rates in office properties in many major cities have exploded to the upside.

This has collapsed office values in numerous regions across the nation. As but one recent example, Boeing (BA) just sold its former headquarters building in Chicago for $22 million. The building was last sold in 2005 for $168 million. Not surprisingly, given these factors, delinquency rates for commercial mortgage-backed securities, or CMBS, are higher than the peak during the Great Financial Crisis. And this doesn't capture the impact of loan modifications in this space, also known as 'extend and pretend.'

New York City has done a solid job of converting older office buildings into much-needed residential buildings, but office vacancy rates remain at/near historical highs in places like San Francisco, Houston, and Los Angeles. Eventually the implosion in office values will have to show up on the balance sheets of CRE lenders in the form of increased write-offs and credit losses. Of note, regional banks originate roughly 70% of CRE loans and hold approximately 30% of CRE debt.

The problems across the office sector will be headwinds to the CRE sector, the regional banking system, and marginally to the U.S. economy for at least the next few years, in my view. And loans against office properties make up roughly $1.3 trillion of the approximately $4.8 trillion CRE debt outstanding in the United States.

However, in today's column I want to focus on the multi-family sector. This is a much larger slice of the CRE market, accounting for around 45% of all CRE debt outstanding. In addition, the deterioration in this CRE subsector is not getting nearly enough ink in the financial press. CMBS delinquency rates for multi-family properties have more than doubled over the past 15 months to right around seven percent.

In addition, the fundamentals for the sector grew notably worse in 2025. This is primarily due to a toxic combination. The first part of the dismal brew was a surge of apartments that came online from 2023 through 2025, helped greatly by the Fed pushing down rates to near zero following Covid. The second part of this increasingly perfect storm was the mass re-migration that occurred in 2025. Over 2.5 million migrants left the U.S., most via self-deportation. Given the recent surge of funding for increased immigration and border enforcement, this trend will continue to play out in 2026.

And the majority of the increase in renter households from 2022 to 2024 came via the surge in migration under the previous administration. The combination of these two factors is playing out in the rental sector. Average national rents have fallen on a year-over-year basis for five straight months now.

For landlords the situation continues to deteriorate, especially in the Sunbelt states. This was where the majority of new apartment construction took place in recent years in response to the surge of people into the region following the pandemic.

More and more rental complexes are turning to the increasing utilization of incentives to move growing inventory. A study by Apartment List found Phoenix led the major regions using concessions of at least one month free on a new lease at 54%. The national multifamily vacancy rate also hit 7.2% in November. The highest level since Apartment List began keeping records in 2017.

Obviously, falling rents and increasing concession usage are huge negatives for any landlord's profit margins. Especially as costs such as insurance, maintenance costs, property taxes, etc. continue to rise at a healthy clip. This is a growing headwind for large apartment REITs like Camden Property Trust (CPT). REITs concentrated in the Sunbelt, like Mid-America Apartments (MAA), are particularly vulnerable.

Given these dynamics, I would expect delinquency rates on multi-family loans to continue to rise, as will credit losses. This is obviously a negative for CRE lenders such as pension funds, insurance companies, and regional banks. The deteriorating rental environment will also be hard on 'mom and pop' landlords that make up the majority of the investor base in single family homes, not to mention duplexes and triplexes. I would expect an increase of these types of properties hitting the market as they no longer provide an adequate return. This is the last thing the housing market needs. I highlighted why housing is likely to experience another challenging year in 2026 in this recent article.

Ending on a much brighter note, falling rents are a blessing for rental households and should positively impact disposable incomes among this cohort. Hopefully, this boosts consumer sentiment at least marginally. And this consumer reading is quite dismal, especially given robust GDP growth.

Finally, and perhaps most importantly, is that falling rents are hugely positive on the inflation front. The 'shelter component' is by far the largest component of the CPI calculation, which came in slightly lower than expected Tuesday. Specifically, the CPI for Owners Equivalent of Rent, or OER, makes up 26.4% of the weighting of the all-items CPI. OER also accounts for 33% of core CPI and 44% of core services CPI.

And that is our look at the current state of the multi-family landscape and the current environment's impact across the U.S. economy as 2026 gets underway.

data.title: VICI Properties: Why I'm Buying At These Lows Even With The Caesars Specter
data.description:
VICI Properties Inc. (VICI) has realized a sustained dip in its stock price due to uncertainty from raised analyst concerns around rent paid by Caesars Entertainment (CZR), its largest tenant. This has been overlaid with an expansion of long-term U.S. Treasury yields and the well-publicized slowdown in Las Vegas tourism. Visitors to the city in November dropped by 5.2% year-over-year, with market anxiety coalescing around whether this fall is temporary or the early innings in the formation of a longer-term trend. The common shares are down 18.2% from their 52-week high, placing VICI on a more than 1-year low and opening up an opportunity to expand a position in the gaming, hospitality, and entertainment-focused REIT.

Critically, a series of analyst downgrades, including a note from J.P. Morgan (JPM) analyst Daniel Politzer that raised the possibility of a rent reduction being granted to CZR, has ramped up the perceived risk profile of the investment-grade rated REIT. CZR formed 39% of annualized base rent ("ABR") as of the end of VICI's fiscal 2025 third quarter. A resolution around this raised possibility presents a clear catalyst for either upside or further downside. CZR is paying roughly $730 million in rent for the regional casinos it leases from VICI, with these generating roughly $750 million in annual cash flow, according to JPM. VICI was actually spun off from CZR back in late 2017, with its current roster of tenants subject to long-term triple-net leases with CPI-linked annual rent escalators. Hence, any possible narrow rent coverage could be an issue compounded every year.

VICI owned 93 properties, formed from 54 gaming assets and 39 other experiential properties as of the end of the third quarter. This has been expanded over the years, with VICI taking a positive approach to acquisitions. The imperative is now on management to reduce the CZR concentration while maintaining adjusted funds from operations, or AFFO, and dividend growth. VICI last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.45 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1.80 per share annualized for a 6.47% dividend yield. This yield has expanded to its highest level in well over 5 years, a conundrum against its 5-year compound annual growth rate of 7.06%.

The risk here, and worst-case scenario, for income investors would be the JPM analyst concerns playing out. This would force down revenue and AFFO, albeit a highly unlikely event, as VICI is a landlord and does not have the same operational risk as a casino operator. The sentiment around slowing Las Vegas numbers seems misplaced around this fact, VICI gets paid regardless of numbers.

Revenues, FFO, And Golden Entertainment

VICI generated third-quarter revenue of $1 billion, up 3.7% from its year-ago comp and in line with consensus estimates. Adjusted FFO ("AFFO") was $0.60 per share, beating consensus by 1 cent and growing from $0.57 per share a year ago. The REIT also hiked its guidance for full-year 2025 AFFO to $2.36 to $2.37 per share, up at the bottom end from prior guidance of $2.35 per share. This means VICI is swapping hands for an 11.92x multiple to the low end of its AFFO guidance. Hence, total returns for current shareholders will be driven by the aggregate of AFFO multiple expansion, the dividend yield, and future dividend hikes. The CZR-led selloff has also ramped up the importance of the recent sale-leaseback with Golden Entertainment (GDEN).

The sale will see GDEN shareholders receive 0.902 shares of VICI for seven casinos, with VICI also set to assume and repay up to $426 million of GDEN's outstanding debt. GDEN will dilute CZR by 100 basis points to 38% of total cash rent and will bring in at least $87 million in its first year, but is subject to annual rent escalators from the third year of the lease. The deal with GDEN also opens up speculation about a deal with casino REIT Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI). GLPI generated third-quarter revenue of $397.61 million, up 3.2% over its year-ago comp, with guidance for full-year 2025 AFFO at $3.86 to $3.88 per share. This means a multiple of 11.5x.

Conclusion

The uncertainty of a long position in VICI now centers on what the resolution to the raised CZR headwinds will be. A rent reduction is a non-starter, even against AFFO coverage of 131%. CZR is also an incredibly profitable company, generating free cash flow of $122 million in its last reported quarter, with a total cash position of $836 million. This is a headwind that really should not have materialized, as CZR is a strong tenant with a healthy credit profile and also repurchasing $400 million of its common stock since the summer of 2024. CZR's management stated during their third-quarter earnings call that this has shrunk their share base by 6%.

VICI properties now offers its highest dividend yield in more than 5 years, with strong AFFO coverage over its dividend and a stock price that is trading close to its 52-week low, even after an aggregate of 75 basis points of rate cuts. VICI will likely be more acquisitive in 2026, with its investment-grade balance sheet holding $3.1 billion in liquidity as of the end of its third quarter before the GDEN sale-leaseback transaction. However, the market is no longer willing to ignore its concentration risks after the raised CZR headwinds, and VICI will need to more aggressively diversify its revenues. I am rating the ticker as a Buy.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: A Solid Hold Even If Venezuela Is 'Uninvestable'
data.description:
Exxon Mobil, oil prices, and Venezuela

I last covered ExxonMobil Corporation (NYSE:XOM) 11-28-2025 with an comparative analysis against Occidental Petroleum (OXY). The analysis was prompted by the latest dividend changes from both companies and rated XOM as hold. Since then, a few material developments have evolved around both XOM stock and also the oil industry as a whole. In particular ,this article will share my thoughts on the development Venezuela. Key developments relevant to XOM are quoted below for readers unfamiliar with the background.

Seeking Alpha News (Jan 13): Exxon Mobil (XOM) has signaled continued interest in sending an assessment team to Venezuela for evaluating oil infrastructure and other assets. The report comes days after U.S. President Donald Trump reached out to executives from more than 20 U.S. energy companies in a live-streamed meeting Friday, with Exxon CEO Darren Woods saying that Venezuela is “uninvestable” without significant changes to the country's commercial frameworks, legal system, and hydrocarbon laws.

In the remainder of this section, I will A) explore how these developments could translate into sizable impacts for both XOM and the whole oil sector, and B) explain why XOM remains a solid investment idea even if Venezuela remains uninvestable (or uninvestable for XOM).

To start, I expect the developments in Venezuela to have long lasting impact on oil prices given the huge reserves and production potential in the country (more on this in the next section). As a background, oil prices have been already pressured lately due to the supply-demand dynamics. As shown in the first chart below, crude oil future prices have retreated from around ~$80 to the current level of $61 only in the past year, a decline of more than 20%. Brent crude oil prices declined even more, with a 23.5% pullback in the past year. Against this background, I see several reasons for oil prices to remain pressured at or even below $60 in 2026, as detailed below.

Factors that keep oil prices compressed

As just mentioned, I anticipate the developments in Venezuela to further compound the oil supply-demand issue due to its reserve and production potential. As shown in the chart below, Venezuela’s oil reserve is estimated to be about 300 billion barrels, even more than Saudi Arabia (about 267 billion) and far above the US (about 35 billion). Overall, Venezuela’s oil reserve accounts for about 20% of global reserve.

However, in contrast to its huge reserve, Venezuela’s oil production only accounts for less than 1% of global crude oil production currently, mostly due to years of political instability, lack of infrastructure improvements, and lack of capital and technology infusion as I see it. With the going political change in the country and the Trump administration’s involvement, I see large odds for these factors to change in the near-term. Especially given the geographical proximity and also many US oil companies existing presence in the country already (such as Chevron), I see large potential for the country’s oil production to ramp up and add to the global supply.

XOM stock: profit impacts from oil prices

Despite its integrated business model, I anticipate XOM’s bottom-line to be impacted materially by compressed oil prices. As an example, the next chart displays my back-of-the-envelope estimation of such impact. This estimation is based on a few assumptions: A) a daily production rate of 3.0M barrels per day based on an average of XOM’s recent throughput capacity, B) a break-even production price of $45 per barrel, and B) a share count that remains constant at its current level. Under the above parameters, the end result is that I see an EPS impact of about $0.25 per share for every $1 of oil price decline.

Based on the above estimated EPS impacts, the next table shows my projected share price changes for XOM. At the current oil price and share prices (of around $128), XOM’s FWD EPS estimate is around $6.95 and its current FWD P/E multiples are around 18.2x. Looking ahead, the most likely combinations I see are a P/E ratio of around 15x (its historical median is around 16x in the past 9 years) and an oil price in the range of $60 to $65. Under these combinations, the projected share prices fall in the range of $104 to $124 as highlighted in the chart. With an FWD dividend rate of $4.12 per share, I see a quite balanced return/risk curve as such.

Other risks and final thoughts

A few other downside risks are worth mentioning besides XOM’s uncertainty participation in Venezuela. To start, XOM shares are currently priced at a sizable premium either by its own historical norm or relative to the sector median. As you can tell from the following chart, its current TTM P/E is about 18.2x, about 1/3 above the sector median of 13.67x and more than 45% above its own 5-year average of 12.48x. Expanding the timeframe a bit, the historical median P/E is ~16x in the past 9 years as just mentioned. In terms of business fundamentals, even if XOM becomes a meaningful participation in Venezuela, there are a series of production issues the company has to resolve, which can require sustained CAPEX investments from XOM for years to come.

On the upside, there are always a few factors that can support higher oil prices and better profitability for XOM. As mentioned in a recent article, per the latest EIA data, the current status of the U.S.’s strategic petroleum reserve (“SPR”) is among the lowest levels since the 1980s and the Trump administration has been gradually refilling the SPR. Also, the OPEC+ countries have announced plans to pause production hikes to stabilize oil prices (see more details from the report and chart below). Finally, if XOM does become a meaningful participant in Venezuela, the participation could meaningfully boost XOM’s throughput and also potentially lower its break-even production prices. Furthermore, Venezuelan crude is a high-sulfur heavy crude oil, which happens to match the processing capacity of many of XOM’s refineries in my assessment and further brightens the upside.

Reuters report: “Global economic activity is expected to maintain its strong performance in both 2026 and 2027," OPEC said in the report. The OPEC+ group comprising OPEC nations, plus Russia and other allies, began raising oil output last year after years of output cuts, and plans to pause production hikes in the first quarter of 2026, amid widespread predictions of oversupply.

To close, this follow-up article on XOM is triggered mostly by the ongoing uncertainties in Venezuela. These uncertainties can both impact global oil price and also XOM’s specific operations. After examining the potential impacts, I see a well-balanced return/risk curve for XOM shares under current conditions and reiterate my hold rating.

data.title: Banner Corporation: As Earnings Near, I Remain Bullish
data.description:
Over the last few years now, I've gotten more interested in the financial sector. It was, for much of my investment career, an area that I did not tread in often. But it was actually the banking crisis of 2022 that led me to finally take the dive and see what all was going on deep down. It has been a fascinating journey since then, and many of the calls that I have made have played out well.

One decent example of this can be seen by looking at Banner Corporation (BANR), a bank with a market capitalization as of this writing of $2.19 billion. Although the stock is down 3.2% since I reaffirmed it as a “Buy” in September 2025, which is far worse than the 4.8% increase that the S&P 500 has experienced, since I originally rated it a “Buy” in September 2023, shares are up 58.6%. By comparison, the market is up a slightly more modest but still impressive 54.2%.

Even though the stock has fallen in recent months, I remain a believer in it and its potential. To be clear, I do think the easy money has been made. But I was not ready late last year to downgrade it. And looking at the data again, I feel similarly. In fact, with earnings around the corner, with management expected to announce results after the market closes on Jan. 21, I think that there is the opportunity for some more upside. This is because, in addition to improvements that management has made over time, the business is also expected to enjoy rising revenue and profits. So unless something else changes, I believe that maintaining a soft “Buy” rating is the right choice.

Not ready to stop banking on Banner

Before we get into the estimates that analysts have for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year, I think it would be helpful to check out how Banner Corporation has performed as of late. The most recent quarter for which we have data available is the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Management reported deposits during that time of $14.02 billion. That's up nicely from the $13.53 billion the company had in the second quarter, and it compares favorably to the $13.51 billion the business ended the final quarter of 2024 with.

Usually when I see this much of a move higher in such a short window of time, I look at whether a surge in brokered deposits occurred. This is not necessarily a bad thing, but it helps to understand whether growth has been organic or caused by something else. After all, brokered deposits are high-cost deposits borrowed from other financial institutions. According to management, however, the company only had $50 million worth of brokered deposits during the most recent quarter. That's a dip from the $50.3 million reported the previous year. In essence, these are statistically insignificant. The real growth for the company came from regular savings accounts, which grew from $3.48 billion to $3.71 billion, and interest-bearing checking accounts that expanded from $2.39 billion to $2.73 billion. Overall, this appears healthy.

As deposits for the company increased, the value of loans also grew. The firm went from $11.20 billion worth at the end of 2024 to $11.54 billion worth in the third quarter of last year. The most significant exposure that the institution has when it comes to loans would be the $1.65 billion in investment properties that fall under the commercial real estate category. However, the company also has significant exposure when it comes to other areas as well, like owner-occupied real estate, small-balance commercial real estate, commercial business loans, small business scored loans, and one- to four-family residential properties. Each of these is in excess of $1 billion.

Other aspects of the balance sheet should not be ignored. According to management, the firm ended the most recent quarter with $2.99 billion of deposits. That is actually down from the $3.11 billion reported the previous year. Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalents went from $501.9 million to $672.9 million. And debt went from $563 million down to $296.8 million. This all paints an interesting picture. What this shows is that management has decided to bolster its cash position while reducing securities and growing its loan balances slower so that it can pay off debt. This is almost certainly because of a desire to boost profit margins. And as you will see in a moment, that is precisely what we have experienced.

When it comes to the income statement, things are going swimmingly well. The third quarter of 2025 saw net interest income for the company of $147.3 million. That's up from $134 million reported a year earlier. An increase in the size of the company's balance sheet contributed to this. But there was another driver as well. As interest rates are falling, the amount that banks have to pay depositors declines. This has permitted an increase in net interest margin from 3.72% to 3.98%. To think that the picture would have been even better had it not been for the fact that the provision for credit losses for the company grew from $1.7 million to $2.7 million. But I digress.

Non-interest income has also been growing. It expanded from $18.1 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $20.7 million in the third quarter of 2025. This was primarily thanks to an increase in miscellaneous income from $1.7 million to $3.2 million, followed by a growth in BOLI income from $2.4 million to $2.7 million. For context, BOLI is what management refers to as bank-owned life insurance. Regardless of the reason, this helped to push net profits up to $53.5 million compared to the $45.2 million reported the previous year. And the $144.1 million generated in profits during the first nine months of 2025 exceeded the $122.5 million reported a year earlier.

Assuming that the final quarter of 2025 looks a lot like the rest of the year has so far, we are looking at a company that's trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 11.1. This is, unfortunately, above the 10 maximum that I typically prefer. But as the chart above illustrates, Banner Corporation is cheaper than five other banks that I compared it to. In the chart below, you can see a similar ranking when it comes to the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. In both of these instances, only one of the institutions that I looked at ended up being cheaper than it is.

This relative valuation certainly has played a role in my decision to remain bullish. But that on its own would not be sufficient. I am also drawn by the fact that asset quality is high. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 1.30%. I typically prefer this to be 1% or greater. This also puts the institution above four of the five banks that I compared it to. And in the subsequent chart, you can see a return on equity rating of 11.33%. My preference is for this to be 10% or greater. So the firm does pass on that. On top of this, it is superior to these other five banks that I compared it to. So asset quality, on the whole, looks great.

We should also touch, even if only briefly, on credit quality metrics. And here is another area where Banner Corporation shines. The company ended the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year with only 0.27% of its assets classified as non-performing. This is admittedly up from the 0.24% that we saw at the end of the previous fiscal year. But it's comfortably below the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see. Similarly, only 0.33% of its loans were classified as being non-accrual during that time. That's barely any different from the 0.32% reported at the end of 2024. And, once again, it is below the 0.50% maximum I like to look at.

At the start of this article, I did mention that management will be reporting financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on Jan. 2. The good news is that, leading up to that point, analysts seem quite bullish. The firm is expected to report revenue of $173.2 million. This is synonymous with the sum of net interest income without factoring in the provision for credit losses and non-interest income. During the final quarter of 2024, revenue defined this way came in at $160.5 million. So analysts expect a rather nice increase year over year. With that, earnings per share are expected to climb from $1.34 to $1.45. This would mean an increase in net profits from $46.4 million to $50.3 million year over year.

Takeaway

As things stand, it is my belief that Banner Corporation is an attractive prospect. It's not as cheap as it once was, and the upside is limited because of that. But asset quality is high, and credit quality metrics are solid. The balance sheet and the income statement continue to show year-over-year progress. And on top of this, the company is cheap relative to other similar firms. When you add all of this together, I do believe that a soft "Buy" rating is appropriate.

data.title: MoneyShow's Best Investment Ideas For 2026: Part 4
data.description:
MoneyShow’s top contributing analysts, strategists, and newsletter editors share their investment recommendations for the year ahead. See Part 3 here.

Eli Lilly & Co.

By Mike Cintolo, Chief Investment Strategist at Cabot Wealth Network

Eli Lilly & Co. (LLY) needs little introduction, as it’s probably the most popular drug stock in the world right now thanks to Mounjaro (diabetes) and Zepbound (weight loss), which are the same drug selling well under both indications. But we still think the stock can surprise on the upside going forward.

In Q3 2025, Mounjaro’s sales lifted 109% and Zepbound’s boomed 185%. Combined, they made up 57% of total revenue, which itself grew 54% from the year before.

Of course, the success of these drugs has been well known for a while, and in the market, that means a lot has been discounted.
